title: Feature Request: Add support for measurement units in ReprHighlighter
body:
Currently, ReprHighlighter handles numeric literals well, but it treats trailing measurement units (like GB, ms, %) as plain text. This makes technical logs and performance metrics harder to scan quickly, as the unit doesn't share the same visual importance or distinct styling as the number it belongs to.

I've developed an enhancement for the number regex in ReprHighlighter to include an optional number_unit named group. This allows Rich to recognize and style common units (TB, GB, MB, KB, B, m

title: [BUG] Typing of highlighter needs improvement
body:
The type annotation of Highlighter.highlights should be `ClassVar[tuple[str | re.pattern[str],...]]` rather than implicit.

Otherwise linters (rightfully) complain that it is bad to use a mutable type (`list`) for a class variable (https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/mutable-class-default/). title: [REQUEST] Remove `typing_extensions` as a run time dependency
body:
**How would you improve Rich?**

Rich currently has run time a dependency on typing_extensions, but this can be removed by moving all the imports into type checking blocks,

e.g.

```python
if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):
    from typing import Self
else:
    from typing_extensions import Self  # pragma: no cover
```

Becomes:

```python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
        # Can be replaced with `from typing import Self` in Python 3.11+

title: [BUG] Behavior of console.print() with empty *args is different from regular print()
body:
**Describe the bug**

Expected that `console.print(end="!")` will print only `!` as regular `python print()`, but it print line break without ending symbol.

```python
from rich.console import Console

console = Console()

for f in (
    lambda: print("A", end="!"),
    lambda: print(end="!"),
    lambda: console.print("A", end="!"),
    lambda: console.print(end="!"),
):
    print("=>", end="")
    f()
    print("<=")
```

Output:
```
❯ python3 test.py
=>A!<=
=>!<=
=>A!<=
=>
<=
```

rich == 14.

```python
from rich.progress import tqdm_track
import time

for i in tqdm_track(range(10), description="One"):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    for j in tqdm_track

'))

> Consider Documentation
> Consider whether the change you made would benefit from documentation - if the feature has any user impact at all, the answer is almost certainly yes! Documentation can be found in the docs directory. There are some additional dependencies required to build the documentation. These dependencies can be installed by running (from the docs directory):
> `pip install -r requirements.txt`

from rich.progress import Progress
import time

with Progress() as progress:

    def __exit__() -> None:

title: rich.pretty.install does not work for IPython
body:
Version:
```
Python 3.8.13

  try:
      ip = get_ipython()  # type: ignore[name-defined]

title: [BUG] Wrong encoding in Windows CMD or Powershell redirection
body:
**Describe the bug**

**Problem**: `Rich` uses the wrong `stdout.encoding` in Windows when the underlining `stdout` is a [`FileIO`][fio] (this is the default mode for [CMD/PowerShell redirection][rd] such as `pip > out.txt`). In most cases, the program crashes with `UnicodeEncodeError` or prints out Mojibake.

```

title: Allow Custom Terminator for RichHandler [REQUEST]
body:
Currently, the `RichHandler` emits through `self.console.print(log_renderable)`. 
By default, the `console.print` function ends the string with `"\n"`, and this is not modifiable through the handler from the user end.

In contrast, native python logging handlers use the `handler.terminator` property to determine how to end the string. By default, this property is `"\n"`, but can be modified directly to change the behavior of the handler. (see [here](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.h

**How would you improve Rich?**

title: [REQUEST] Allow delimiters when using `pprint(..., max_depth=...)`
body:
**How would you improve Rich?**

Currently, when pretty printing a nested object, we can set the max depth to simplify the object display:
```python
>>> from rich.pretty import pprint
>>> pprint({"a": {"b": {"c": "d"}}, "e": ["f", "g"]}, max_depth=1)
{'a': ..., 'e': ...}
```

However, by using ellipsis like this, we obscure the information about the data type of "a". In contrast, Python's built-in `pprint` module adds delimiters around the ellipsis, to differentiate between a list and a

title: [BUG] rich.pretty.pprint is too eager to highlight a closing >
body:
**Describe the bug**

Given this code:

```python
from rich.pretty import pprint as print

class TestClass:

    def __repr__( self ) -> str:
        return '<Outer: some: "value", plus: <Some: other: "child object">, and: 42>'

print( TestClass() )
```

`pprint` applies bold to the first instance of `<` and the first instance of '>', resulting in an unbalanced pair of highlights if there is another instance of a '</>'-wrapped object in the `__repr__` return value.

title: Style.render cache
body:
The Style.render method is very cachable. It's tempting to add a @lru_cache and be done with it. The problem is that may use up a significant amount of memory since the string sizes could be arbitrarily large.

However the performance win is too tempting to overlook, and will have the greatest benefit for Textual.

I think we should have a `render_cache` on the Console object that is disable by default, but in Textual we can set that to an LRUCache.

title: Added `echo` bool to capture.
body:
The `capture` method should have an `echo` parameter. When `echo=True` (the default) text should be captured and written to the terminal. When `echo=False` the text should be captured but not written to the terminal.

See https://github.com/Textualize/rich/issues/2172 for context.

title: [BUG] Wrong terminal size when piping to a Rich app
body:
When using a Console between two pipes, i.e `git diff | dunk | less -R` The output width is not the full width of the terminal by default.

Inside `dunk`, it's worked around by doing `Console(force_terminal=True, width=os.get_terminal_size(2))` which results in the content that gets piped into less being the whole terminal width.

title: embedding Rich SVGs leaks styles
body:
When embedding SVGs within a web page, the SVG styles "leak" in to the HTML. For instance the span style changes in the rest of the page.

I'm not sure if there is some easy way to prevent that, but if not, perhaps we should generate a random ID for the SVG and update the styles to only use that UD.

```python

log = logging.g

title: [BUG] Should RichHandler default to stderr
body:
**Describe the bug**

"Bug" is a little strong, but I noticed that by default Python logging writes to stderr, but when using RichHandler by default it writes to stdout.

I know you can get around this with something like:

``` python
logging.basicConfig(
    level="DEBUG", datefmt="[%X]", handlers=[RichHandler(console=Console(stderr=True))]
)
```

But would it be more sensible and less surprising for new users if by default RichHandler logged to stderr?

title: [BUG] rich.traceback: no lexer for filename X found

**Describe the bug**

The rich.traceback doesn't know how to render tracebacks originating from inside jinja2 templates.

title: [REQUEST] Add explicit support for NamedTuples
body:
The Pretty class doesn't support pretty printing NamedTuples.

We should attempt to auto detect named tuples in order to expand these on to multiple lines as appropriate.

NamedTuples inherit from `tuple` and don't have a common base class. They have a `_fields` attribute which could identify them as a named tuple. 

We will also need to check if a custom __repr__ has been provided (in the same was as we do dataclasses).

title: Replace Colorama with internal solution for windows legacy mode
body:
Colorama is a wonderful hack that wraps stdout to process ansi codes. It is problematic today because it is essentially a global setting and interferes with the ability to enable ansi escape sequences with newer Windows.

We should replace Colorama with equivalent functionality within Rich itself that generates the appropriate win32 api calls. This will hopefully be a little faster, and give us much more granular control over when to enable it.

I think we can do this at the Segment level. I

title: [BUG] `rich.prompt.PromptBase.make_prompt` raises a `TypeError` when `choices` are not a `list[str]`
body:
**Describe the bug**

The bug is related with the `choices` argument of the `PromptBase.ask` function. Concretely, when the `PromptBase` implementation has a `response_type` distinct from `str`, it's not possible to fill the `choices` argument as it is expected to be a `list[str]`. So, cannot be any valid value to be passed (as it must be of `response_type` type and also must be in `choices`, but if, for example,  `response_type` is `int` and `choices` is a `list[str]` then the condition can n

title: [BUG] Please relax typing-extensions versions
body:
**Describe the bug**
The recent `typing-extensions` release is `4.0`. It is backward compatible, a team decided to use SemVer notation for the project.
rich is marked as compatible with `^3.7.4` which doesn't sail smoothly with other libraries.

Maybe the best solution is not pinning the top version and relay on `typing-extensions` stability.
Another option is pinning to `>=3.7.4, <5.0`.

I've tried to prepare a PR but found that `poetry lock` generates a very different lockfile than push

title: [REQUEST] Why is "meta serialization" 100% required and can it be got rid of or replaced with something less dangerous than `pickle` and `marshal`?
body:
**How would you improve Rich?**

Having `rich` installed on a machine would feel more secure.

I saw that `rich` uses dangerous modules (currently, `marshal`). For usage of such dangerous modules an enough strong justification is needed.

I saw it used for so called `meta serialization`, but I haven't found any docs describing what is it and why it is 100% needed and why `marshal` is needed for it.

title: [BUG] rich.spinner doesn't work
[BUG] Deadlock when exiting Status context
body:
**Describe the bug**

rich appears to run into a deadlock when exiting from a `Status` or `Live` context. This appears to be analogous to #90. The analysis in that issue appears to apply here, as well: The refresh thread is joined under lock. This means that the refresh thread may be waiting for the lock, while the main thread holds the lock waiting for the refresh thread to exit.

**To Reproduce**

```python
import itertools
import random
import time
from rich.console import Console

title: [BUG] Conflict between rich progress and click.echo()
body:
There is a conflict between `rich.progress.Progress` and `click.echo()`.

The `rich.progress.Progress` context manager monkey-patches `sys.stdout`. `click.echo()` detects whether `sys.stdout` is a text stream or byte stream (this is questionable, but it is a different issue) by trying to write an empty bytes object. If the test is passed successfully, then it is a bytes stream, and click encodes string before writing it. But the wrapper instantiated by Progress ignores empty input for `write()

title: Pyton ufa
[BUG] word joiner characters can break table row layout
body:
**Describe the bug**
When the text in a table has [word joiner characters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_joiner) in it, the width of the row can be miscalculated, causing borders to overflow to the next line.

**To Reproduce**
```
from rich.console import Console
from rich.table import Table

console = Console()

title: [BUG] Pretty printing can break tuples containing a single item when expanding
body:
When pretty printing a tuple with one item, the pretty printing omits the comma necessary to make it a tuple.

For instance if you pretty print `(1,)` with `expand_all=True`, you get this:

```
(
    1
)
```

Which is no longer a tuple. If the tuple is not expanded then the result is `(1,)` which is correct.

```python

title: [BUG] Typo in repr.ipv4 regex?
body:
**Describe the bug**
In the `ReprHighlighter` class, there seems to be a typo in the regex for IPV4 addresses:
`r"(?P<ipv4>[0-9]{1,3}\.[0-9]{1,3}\.[0-gt9]{1,3}\.[0-9]{1,3})",`
The third octet will also accept the letters 'g' and 't', which causes strings like '192.168.ggt.1' to be highlighted.

**To Reproduce**
```python
>>> from rich.console import Console
>>> c = Console()
>>> c.print('192.168.1.1')
192.168.1.1
>>> c.print('192.168.t.1')
192.168.t.1
>>> c.print('192.168.gt.1')
19

title: [REQUEST] Respect PY_COLORS environment variable
body:
`PY_COLORS` is a [very popular](https://github.com/search?l=&q=PY_COLORS&type=code) method for forcing ANSI mode on or off in a command line application. 

Basically undefined means keeping application default behavior, likely auto-detection. Having it `PY_COLORS=0` means application will not use any ANSI codes and `PY_COLORS=1` will force ANSI mode regardless if terminal reports it or not.

title: [REQUEST] rich.print != rich.console.print
body:
Putting `from rich import print` at the top of your imports is a nice quick way to load in the power of rich without setting up a console and all that.

However, this version of `print` does not expose all of the arguments one gets from `console.print`. `rich.print` provides `sep`, `end`, `file`, and `flush`--presumably because this is intended as a drop-in replacement for Python's own `print` function.

title: Request: do not use WT_SESSION to detect Windows Terminal
body:
There's a small handful of issues with detecting `WT_SESSION` and using that as an indicator that you can use advanced VT:

* `WT_SESSION` is not an API, and it was my greatest fear when we introduced it that people would use it for feature detection
   * This is why I (so far) refuse to add support for `TERM_PROGRAM` or `TERM_PROGRAM_VERSION`, and would rather us standardize on `TERM` :smile:
* It _will not_ be set by other terminal emulators using the pseudoconsole API (like Visual Studio'

title: Bug: 24-bit color should work on conhost as of Windows 10.0.15063
body:
According to [this blog post](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/commandline/24-bit-color-in-the-windows-console/), the windows console host added support for 24-bit color in build 14931.

It is safer and easier to gate the use of 24-bit color on the Windows version and _not_ the presence of Windows Terminal, especially as WT is not the only terminal emulator that benefits from conhost enhancements.

title: Text.assemble() can't take strings without styles
body:
The documentation for `Text.assemble()` says:

> The positional arguments should be either strings, or a tuple of string + style.

but it looks like bare strings don't work; the code passes `*part` to `append()` without checking if it's actually a tuple.

title: Add support for hyperlinks
body:
In 2017 some terminals [gained support for hyperlinks](https://gist.github.com/egmontkob/eb114294efbcd5adb1944c9f3cb5feda).

It would be very nice if in addition to the other BBcode-style formats we can use, like `[bold]`, we could also use `[url]`.

Python can output hyperlinks like so: `print("\033]8;;http://example.com\033\\This is a link\033]8;;\033\\")`

An example BBcode-style implementation might be: `[url="http://example.com"]This is a link[/url]`

title: Option for console.print() to print to the specified output stream
body: It would be nice if we could specify the output stream that console.print() should output to. For example, a terminal based program often needs to print to standard error. With vanilla Python it's possible with `print("foo", file=sys.stderr)`. But I didn't find that kind of option in rich.

title: `sep` parameter is inconsistent with builtin `print`
body:
`sep` behaves differently from builtin print. I don't know if this is intentional or not, but the behaviour is slightly different from how the docs describe it. (I didn't expect the `sep` to be added to the *end* of the string, only *between* the printed items).

**Example:**
```python
print(".", ".", sep="a")
console.print(".", ".", sep="a")
```

**Output:**
```
.a.
.a.a
```

**Expected output:**
```
.a.
.a.
```

**Example:**
```python

title: Setting up coverage reporting via CI / linting?
body:
1. Are you interested on having coverage reports via CI?

I suggest using codecov for that (with a config that disables its comments, so that only PR/commit statuses are reported).

I also suggest having a tox configuration for this then, so that `tox -e py38-coverage` could be used on CI, but also locally in the same way.

2. Are you interested in having some linting via CI / tox also?
For that using flake8 might be good enough, but would require some fixes / running black/autopep8 once.

title: Underscores are being stripped out incorrectly in table cells

    table.add_column("location", style="test-location")
    table.add_column("description", style="none")

    table.add_row(outcome, location, f"{description} {reason}")
```

If the `description` contains two underscores, such as in many Python function names, the underscores are removed.
